findings.evidence: def at line 30 uses IsCompl; isTotallyReal_iff (line 35) confirms it unfolds to IsCompl L (L.map J), which includes codisjoint/sup_eq_top (lines 60-66). Basic section variables (lines 18-21) impose only Semiring/AddCommMonoid with arbitrary J. grep: no TotallyReal/totally-real declaration in ./mathlib to defer to.
findings.fix: Rename the predicate to reflect the complement/maximal condition (e.g. IsMaximalTotallyReal), so the name matches its strength; the roadmap's geometric 'totally real boundary conditions' usage is the half-dimensional case where the two coincide and does not justify the bare term for this general module-level complement predicate.
findings.issue: IsTotallyReal J L := IsCompl L (L.map J) bundles disjointness AND spanning (L ⊔ J(L) = ⊤). Standard 'totally real' (e.g. McDuff–Salamon) requires only L ∩ J(L) = 0; L ⊕ J(L) = E is the 'maximal totally real' condition. The Basic section even uses an arbitrary J with no J²=-1, and no section carries a half-dimension hypothesis, so spanning is a genuine extra requirement and the bare name advertises a weaker, more general notion than the predicate actually denotes — hypotheses like sup_eq_top/existsUnique_add read as more general than they are.

summary: Lemma names and the skewSwap reuse accurately describe their conclusions. But the defining predicate IsTotallyReal J L := IsCompl L (L.map J), stated over an arbitrary module with an arbitrary J and no dimension hypothesis, uses the bare standard term for the strictly stronger 'maximal totally real' (complement) condition.
